structure.title: INTRODUCTION
structure.summary:
The excerpt is the opening section of the “Terms and Conditions” governing the use of Stake Properties Limited’s online investment platform (website www.getstake.com and mobile apps). It outlines:

* **Purpose and Scope (Section 1)** – The agreement applies to anyone using the platform to invest in property projects and defines the contractual relationship between the user and Stake. It stresses that users must read and accept the agreement, the General Terms of Use, the Data & Privacy Policy, and the Key Risks document before investing, and that continued platform use constitutes consent.

* **Key Definitions (Section 2)** – A comprehensive glossary of essential terms used throughout the agreement, including:
  * **Acquisition Fee** and **Annual Administrative Fees** (referencing clause 14).
  * **AML Laws** – Anti‑money‑laundering and counter‑terrorism regulations (DFSA rulebook and relevant UAE law).
  * **Associated Costs** (clause 3.4).
  * **Beneficial Assignment Period** – The interim stage when economic benefit transfers before the legal share register is updated.
  * **Cancellation Period** – The first 48 hours after an investment can be cancelled.
  * **Client Money Account** – A segregated Emirates NBD Islamic account holding investor funds separate from Stake’s own money.
  * **Customer Wallet** – The portion of a user’s Stake account reflecting the cash held in the Client Money Account.
  * **Commitment Period** – The timeframe from the start of an investment round until the funding target is reached.
  * **Cooling‑Off Period** – The final 48 hours of the Commitment Period, during which investors may reassess their commitment.
  * **DFSA** – The Dubai Financial Services Authority, the regulatory body overseeing the platform.

Overall, the fragment establishes the legal framework for using the platform, emphasizes mandatory risk acknowledgment, and clarifies the terminology that underpins subsequent clauses on fees, investment processes, and regulatory compliance.

structure.title: DEFINITIONS
structure.summary:
**Document Overview** The excerpt is a segment of the **Terms and Conditions** governing the use of the **Stake Properties Limited** online investment platform (website www.getstake.com and mobile apps). Updated on 20 October 2025, the document sets out the contractual framework between the platform (“Stake”) and its users (“Investors”). **Key Sections Covered** | Section | Main Points | |--------|--------------| | **1. Introduction** | - Establishes that the agreement applies to all investments made through the Platform and to the relationship with Stake.
- Requires investors to read and accept the Agreement, General Terms of Use, Data & Privacy Policy, and Key Risks before using the Platform or investing.
- States that continued use constitutes acceptance. | | **2. Definitions** | Provides precise meanings for more than 50 essential terms used throughout the agreement, including:
• **Investment‑related concepts** – Investment, Investment Round, Investment Period, Investment Term, Funding Target, Commitments, Cancelation, Cooling‑off, Lock‑in periods.
• **Property and product types** – Regular Property, Sharia‑compliant Property, Fix n’ Flip, Fix n’ Lease, Leasing Strategy.
• **Financial elements** – Investor Shares, Total Investment, Estimated ROI/Valuation, Dividend, ROI, Sale Value, Acquisition/Annual Administrative/Performance/Exit Fees, KYC & AML Fees.
• **Legal and compliance terms** – DFSA, DIFC, AML Laws, KYC & AML, Shariah Supervisory Board (SSB), Shariah Audit, Sharia‑compliant Investment, Material Change, Special/ Simple Majority Vote.
• **Platform mechanics** – Stake Account, Customer Wallet, Client Money Account, Beneficial Assignment Period, Economic Rights Holder, Transactional Confirmation, Exit Window, Exit Fee.
• **Corporate structure** – Prescribed Company (PC), Shares, Stake Fees, Contractor, Project Manager. | | **3. General** | Describes how the Platform operates and the relationship between Stake and Investors:
• Stake manages the Platform, offers Investor Shares, and runs Investment Rounds to meet Funding Targets for specific properties.
• Each Investment Round creates a Prescribed Company (PC) that holds title to the acquired property on behalf of investors.
• Independent valuation consultants are engaged to determine fair property values and support price negotiations.
• Sharia‑compliance is verified via a Shariah Compliance Property Assessment Report and the SSB.
• References an attached **Administration Agreement** (Appendix 1) that grants Stake authority to administer each PC. | **Overall Focus** The document establishes the legal foundation for participating in Stake’s property‑investment crowdfunding model, clarifying investor rights, responsibilities, fees, timelines, and compliance requirements (including AML/KYC and Shariah rules). It defines the terminology necessary to interpret subsequent clauses (e.g., fees, exit mechanisms, voting thresholds) and outlines the basic operational process of launching investment rounds, acquiring properties, and managing investor interests through the Platform.

structure.title: GENERAL
structure.summary:
The excerpt is a section of Stake Properties Limited’ terms and conditions that outlines key definitions, the structure of the crowdfunding platform, and the mechanics of investing in property projects.

* **Definitions (clauses 2.54‑2.63)** – Provides precise meanings for terms such as the Shariah‑compliance assessment report, the Shariah Supervisory Board (SSB), different voting thresholds (simple and special majority), Stake’s corporate identity, Stake Account, Stake Fees, Total Investment, Transactional Confirmation, and the parties (“You”, “Investors”).  

* **General Platform Framework (clause 3)** – Describes Stake’s role in operating the online platform, presenting properties for subscription of investor shares within a Property Company (PC). It explains how investment rounds are launched, the use of independent valuation consultants, and the mandatory Shariah‑compliance review by the SSB. It also details the disclosure of funding targets and associated costs, the investor’s obligation to fund the acquisition, the process for achieving (or not achieving) the funding target, and the handling of refunds, foreign‑exchange charges, and withdrawals. A disclaimer notes that Stake does not guarantee the accuracy of financial projections posted on the platform.

* **Investment Terms (clause 4)** – Sets out the investor’s agreement to invest via the platform, specifying the investment period, the voting rights concerning the sale of the property after the period, and the distinction between investments made before and after 5 July 2024. It also introduces the appointment of a third‑party Property Manager under the Administration Agreement.  

Overall, the document establishes the legal and operational foundations for Stake’s property‑investment crowdfunding service, emphasizing Shariah compliance, investor rights, fee structures, and procedural steps for funding, acquisition, and exit.

structure.title: TERMS OF YOUR INVESTMENT
structure.summary:
The excerpt is a detailed part of Stake’s investor agreement and platform terms. It explains how Stake ensures that each property product is Sharia‑h compliant and outlines the presentation of funding targets and “Associated Costs” on the platform. It sets out the investor’s obligations to subscribe for Investor Shares, pay all associated costs, and acknowledges that investments are used to acquire the property. The document describes the consequences if a funding target is not reached – refunds (minus any foreign‑exchange charges) and the ability to reinvest or withdraw funds – and clarifies that Stake is not liable for the accuracy of financial projections.

The “Terms of Your Investment” section defines the investment period, voting rights, and how properties listed before 5 July 2024 require a mandatory investor vote on sale, whereas later listings do not (though investors can still initiate a vote). It details the role of a third‑party Property Manager, the process for modifying the investment period, and the voting triggers when a “Threshold ROI” (>50 % of purchase price) is hit or a bona‑fide buyer is found. Minimum and maximum investment amounts are prescribed (minimum AED 500, maximum 33 % of the funding target, with further caps for retail clients), and each Investor Share equals one AED with one vote; fractional shares are prohibited.

The agreement also covers material changes during the commitment period, the optional “Exit Window” liquidity mechanism for buying and selling Investor Shares, and the rights and obligations of buyers and sellers during such transfers.

Finally, the document introduces the two renovation‑backed investment types—Fix n Flip (buy, renovate, sell) and Fix n Lease (renovate and hold for rental income)—detailing similar share structures, voting rights, cost responsibilities, and material‑change procedures for these projects. Throughout, Stake reserves discretion to cancel rounds, alter funding targets, or reject investments, and it emphasizes that platform information is for general consumption and not investment advice.

structure.title: INVESTMENT ROUND
structure.summary:
The excerpt outlines the key terms and procedures governing investments in real‑estate projects on the Stake platform. It explains how investors purchase “Investor Shares” of a property‑holding company (PC), how each investor’s percentage ownership is calculated, and the conditions under which a funding round is deemed complete (reaching the funding target and buying the property).  

It details Stake’s discretionary powers to accept or cancel property listings and investment rounds, as well as its responsibility for interior design, furnishings, and related “Associated Costs.” The document describes the handling of material changes—including notification, voting (special‑majority) and possible cancellation of an investor’s participation—both during and after the commitment period.  

Procedures for selling a property are covered: automatic sale when the disclosed ROI is met, otherwise a special‑majority vote is required; specific rules for “Fix n’ Lease” and “Fix n’ Flip” strategies, including exit windows, sale‑strategy changes, and voting requirements. Stake’s role in conducting due‑diligence, managing third‑party contractors, and overseeing renovations is also outlined.  

The section on the Investment Round explains how properties are listed, the use of independent valuation consultants, and pre‑sale negotiations with sellers.  

Finally, the excerpt introduces the Know‑Your‑Customer (KYC) and Anti‑Money‑Laundering (AML) policy, defining money‑laundering risks, Stake’s AML controls (customer due diligence, a Money‑Laundering Officer, employee training), and the documentation required from investors to verify identity, address, employment and source of funds.

structure.title: KNOW‑YOUR‑CUSTOMER (KYC) AND ANTI‑MONEY LAUNDERING (AML) POLICY
structure.summary:
**Description of the Partial Document**

The excerpt outlines the governance, operational, and regulatory framework for a real‑estate investment platform:

1. **Investment Strategies & Decision‑Making (Sections 4.3.19‑4.3.23)**
   - Details the “Fix n’ Lease” and “Fix n’ Flip” models, including how properties are held, the conditions under which a sale can be triggered, and the need for a Special Majority vote to approve any sale or change in strategy.
   - Explains Stake’s role in conducting due‑diligence, managing conflicts of interest with contractors and project managers, and overseeing renovations.
   - States that Fix n’ Lease properties are eligible for participation in the “Exit Window” (a defined exit mechanism for investors).

2. **Investment Round Process (Section 5)**
   - Describes how Stake operates the platform (website and mobile app) to list identified properties, aggregate investor capital, and reach funding targets as agreed with the seller.
   - Requires an independent valuation by qualified consultants before a property is offered to investors, with the valuation report made available on the platform.
   - Allows Stake to negotiate pre‑sale terms with the seller to maximise investor value.

3. **Know‑Your‑Customer (KYC) and Anti‑Money‑Laundering (AML) Policy (Section 6)**
   - Defines money‑laundering and outlines the legal obligations under AML laws.
   - Sets out Stake’s AML controls: Customer Due Diligence, appointment of an in‑house Money Laundering Reporting Officer, and mandatory AML training.
   - Requires investors to provide identity, address, employment, and source‑of‑funds information; deposits, withdrawals, and investments are blocked until the KYC/AML profile is verified and kept up‑to‑date.

4. **Deposit and Withdrawal Mechanics (Section 7)**
   - Allows deposits in AED via wire transfer or card payment, subject to KYC/AML compliance; cash and third‑party payments are not accepted.
   - Converts any non‑AED deposits at the “as‑received” rate after bank fees.
   - Gives Stake the right to reject non‑compliant deposits or transfers missing correct reference numbers.
   - States that wallet balances earn no interest, remain the investor’s property until invested, and can be withdrawn only after successful KYC/AML checks.
   - Withdrawals are processed to the investor’s bank account in the wallet’s currency; the investor bears all foreign‑exchange, transfer, and bank fees.
   - Clarifies that promotional credits can only be used for investments, not withdrawn as cash.
   - Allows Stake to suspend or restrict accounts for regulatory reasons (e.g., expired ID, outdated address) and mandates that all client funds be held in a segregated Client Money Account under DFSA rules, keeping them separate from Stake’s own funds.

structure.title: DEPOSIT AND WITHDRAWAL
structure.summary:
The excerpt is a section of a user agreement that outlines the rules and procedures for managing a “Stake Account” on the platform. It covers:

* **Deposit rules** – permissible methods (wire transfer or card payment), required KYC/AML verification, currency conversion to AED, rejection rights, and that deposits earn no interest.  
* **Withdrawal rules** – KYC/AML clearance required, funds transferred to a registered bank account, fees and foreign‑exchange costs borne by the user, minimum withdrawal amount, and a processing charge for card‑based withdrawals.  
* **Client‑money safeguards** – segregation of customer funds in a DFSA‑regulated client money account, protection in insolvency, due‑diligence on third‑party banks, and the collective nature of the funds (no individual claim on a specific account).  
* **Account restrictions** – the platform can suspend or restrict accounts for expired ID, address changes, or other compliance issues.  
* **Promotional credit** – any free credit or rewards can only be used for investments, not withdrawn.  
* **Investment process** – funding the wallet, purchasing investor shares in a property, use of a checkout feature for multiple properties, and conditions under which an investment can be cancelled (cooling‑off period, unmet funding target, round discontinuation, etc.), with refunds returned to the wallet for re‑investment or withdrawal.  

Overall, the passage sets out the procedural, compliance, fee, and protection framework governing deposits, withdrawals, and investments on the platform.

structure.title: INVESTMENT PROCESS
structure.summary:
The excerpt outlines key operational and legal aspects of the platform’s handling of client funds and investments:

* **Client‑money safeguards (clauses 7.12‑7.17)** – explains that investors’ money may be held outside the DIFC, subject to the jurisdiction’s insolvency rules, and that the firm conducts due‑diligence on third‑party banks. Funds are pooled with other investors, so no individual claim to a specific account balance exists. Withdrawals are only allowed to a registered bank account in the investor’s name, with processing fees for card‑based withdrawals (up to 2.5 %) and a minimum AED 100 for international transfers.

* **Investment process (clauses 8.1‑8.6)** – describes how investors fund their “Customer Wallet,” select properties, and subscribe to investor shares. It sets out when an investment can be cancelled (e.g., during the cooling‑off period, if the funding target isn’t met, or if the round is discontinued) and what happens to the funds thereafter. After the cooling‑off period the investment is confirmed and used to acquire the property. All amounts are held and distributed in AED, with investors bearing any foreign‑exchange costs.

* **Cancellation rights (clauses 9.1‑9.5)** – gives investors a right to cancel within the cancellation/cooling‑off period, receiving only the original amount (no promotional benefits) and allowing re‑withdrawal or re‑investment subject to KYC/AML. It clarifies that purchases made through an “Exit Window” cannot be cancelled and that the platform may restrict certain properties from that window.

* **Formation of a Prescribed Company (clauses 10.1‑10.2)** – explains that once a funding target is reached, the platform will incorporate a prescribed company (PC) to hold the investors’ funds for a specific property. The PC, initially wholly owned by the platform, will issue investor shares pursuant to the investment, and its administration will be governed by standardized agreements and articles of association, with two named directors appointed at incorporation.

structure.title: CANCELATION RIGHTS
structure.summary:
The excerpt sets out the post‑investment mechanics and investor rights for a property‑investment platform.  

* **Investment Confirmation (8.3‑8.6):** After the cooling‑off period, an investment becomes final and is used to subscribe for Investor Shares in a prescribed company (PC) that will acquire the property. Funds are held in AED; the platform disclaims any liability for foreign‑exchange losses or bank charges, and investors must obtain their own advice on FX, tax or other risks.  

* **Cancellation Rights (Section 9):** Investors may cancel within the cancellation/cooling‑off window via the platform or email, receiving only the original amount (in the investment’s currency) without promotional benefits. Cancelled funds can be returned to the wallet or re‑invested, subject to possible FX charges. Purchases made in an “Exit Window” cannot be cancelled. Stake reserves the right to exclude certain properties from exit windows.  

* **Formation of the Prescribed Company (Section 10):** Once the funding target is met, Stake incorporates a PC to hold the investment, issues Investor Shares, and initially appoints two directors. After incorporation the PC is transferred to the investors, who gain the ability to appoint or remove directors by simple‑majority vote. Investors authorize Stake and the directors to act on the PC’s behalf and agree not to seek direct control of the PC.  

* **Investment Returns (Section 11):** Dividends from rental income are credited to the investor’s wallet, held in a client‑money account with ENBD. Withdrawals or reinvestments are allowed, subject to KYC/AML compliance. Dividend timing depends on rent receipt and may be delayed up to three months; distributions are usually quarterly or monthly, with a minimum credit of AED 0.01. If rent is overdue, the property manager may pursue legal action, with investors voting on the course of action and bearing any legal costs. Investors are solely responsible for taxes and any costs arising from their investment income.

structure.title: FORMATION OF PRESCRIBED COMPANY
structure.summary:
The excerpt outlines the key post‑fund‑raising arrangements and investor rights for a Property Company (PC). It explains that once the funding target is met, the PC will be transferred from the original “Stake” entity to the investors, who can then appoint or remove directors by a simple majority vote. Investors authorize Stake and the PC’s directors to sign all necessary formation and operational documents and agree not to seek direct control of the PC despite owning shares.

The document then details how investment returns are handled: dividends are credited to a customer wallet held in a client‑money account with Emirates NBD Islamic, subject to KYC/AML compliance. Dividend amounts depend on rental income and associated costs, are typically declared quarterly or monthly, may be delayed up to three months for processing, and have a minimum payout of AED 0.01. Overdue rent may trigger a voting process among investors to decide on legal action, with investors bearing any resulting legal costs. Investors are responsible for any taxes on their income.

Finally, the text describes property management and governance mechanisms. An independent property manager, appointed under a separate agreement, handles day‑to‑day operations. Investors are invited to vote on a range of matters—such as approving major works, initiating or approving sales (including “Fix n’ Lease” and “Fix n’ Flip” scenarios), modifying the investment period, changing leasing strategies, and appointing/removing the property manager or PC directors. Each voting item specifies whether a simple or special majority is required, and the process can be initiated via the platform or by contacting the provider.

structure.title: INVESTMENT RETURNS
structure.summary: The excerpt outlines Section 12 of the agreement, detailing how each invested property is overseen and how investors participate in key decisions. It specifies that an independent Property Manager—appointed via a separate management agreement—handles day‑to‑day operations, maintenance, leasing strategy and tenant relations. Major actions (e.g., non‑critical works exceeding 5 % of the original cost, initiating or approving a sale, changing the Property Manager, altering the investment or leasing strategy, or legal actions against tenants) are subject to investor voting, with thresholds ranging from simple majority to special majority as listed in a voting matrix. The process for issuing voting invitations—including required information, voting links, schedules and reminders—is described, and non‑responses are treated as abstentions. All other matters default to a simple‑majority vote. The clause also confirms that a reserve fund will be set aside for maintenance, that expenses over the 5 % threshold require investor approval, and that any unused reserve balances are refunded at term‑end. Finally, it establishes a procedure for investors to request a property sale during the investment period, which triggers a formal voting process to authorize the sale and engage potential buyers.

structure.title: PROPERTY MANAGEMENT, VOTING AND MAINTENANCE
structure.summary:
The excerpt outlines the governance and procedural rules for investors in a jointly‑owned property. It covers:

* **Legal actions** – the right to initiate proceedings against a defaulting tenant or service providers.  
* **Voting framework** – all major decisions (except those expressly listed) are decided by a simple majority. The platform will issue a “Voting Invitation Notice” by email, specifying the matter, supporting documents, voting link, schedule and deadline; non‑responders are treated as abstentions.  
* **Management and tenancy** – the property is run by a designated Property Manager; existing tenants remain in place under the current lease until it is terminated or expires.  
* **Reserve fund** – an amount is set aside at investment to cover maintenance and service costs; excess funds are refunded, and any draw‑down is replenished from rental income. Expenditures above 5 % of the original price require investor approval.  
* **Sale requests** – any investor may submit a Sale Request notice during the investment period. After receipt, a vote is triggered; if approved by simple majority, Stake/PC must seek a bona‑fide buyer offering at least 80 % of the estimated valuation. Failure to find a buyer within 15 days results in automatic rejection of the request.  
* **Sale execution** – once a buyer is identified and an offer made, a final sale vote is held. Approved sales obligate investors to transfer their rights and execute the necessary sale documents, limited to standard seller representations and warranties.  
* **Restrictions on subsequent sale requests** – after a rejected or failed sale request, investors cannot submit another request for 12 months, and no sale requests may be made during the month preceding an “Exit Window.” Properties under a sale vote are excluded from any concurrent exit window.  
* **Investor risk disclaimer** – a reminder that property investments carry the risk of loss and may not achieve expected returns, with reference to a formal risk warning.  

Overall, the passage details how investors are notified, vote, and are bound by decisions regarding property management, maintenance funding, and the sale of the asset.

structure.title: ONGOING STATEMENTS AND VALUATIONS
structure.summary:
**Description of the Partial Document**

The excerpt sets out the rules and procedures governing the sale of a property owned by investors through the platform, the ongoing reporting and valuation of those investments, and the fee structure applied by the platform provider.

1. **Sale‑Request Process (Clauses 12.7.3‑12.9)**
   - After a Sale Request Notice receives a simple‑majority approval, Stake and/or the Project Company (PC) must, in good‑faith, seek a bona‑fide buyer offering at least 80 % of the latest “Estimated Valuation.”
   - If no such buyer is identified within 15 days, the Sale Request is automatically deemed rejected.
   - When a buyer is found, investors are invited to vote on the “Final Sale.” If the vote fails or the request is rejected, the submitting investor (and any other investor) is barred from filing another Sale Request for 12 months.
   - No Sale Requests can be submitted during the month preceding an “Exit Window,” and any property under a sale vote is excluded from that Exit Window.
   - Once a sale is approved, each investor must:
     * Accept the majority decision,
     * Transfer all rights in the property to the buyer, and
     * Execute the necessary conveyance documents, limited to standard seller representations and warranties and a buyer‑only indemnity for any breach.
   - A risk disclaimer reminds investors that property investments may produce losses.

2. **Ongoing Statements and Valuations (Clause 13)**
   - Investors receive regular updates via their Stake dashboard, including realized income/expenses, the latest third‑party “Estimated Valuation,” and any relevant documents (e.g., Sharia‑compliance audit reports).
   - Estimated valuations are refreshed semi‑annually (or at renovation completion for “Fix‑n‑Flip” projects) using a desktop update of the original valuation adjusted for costs, taxes, and recent comparable market data from independent valuers.
   - Annual Sharia‑audit confirmations are provided for Sharia‑compliant investments.

3. **Fee Schedule (Clause 14)**
   - **One‑time transaction fees** charged at acquisition:
     * Acquisition fee = 1.5 % of the Funding Target.
     * Up‑front KYC & AML fee = 0.20 % of the Funding Target.
   - **Exit‑related fees**:
     * Exit fee = 2.5 % of the greater of the Funding Target or the final Sale Value (minimum fee applies from the start).
     * Transfer fee on secondary market moves of Investor Shares = 2.5 % of the Transfer Value, paid by the seller.
   - **Annual fees (deducted pro‑rata from dividend distributions)**:
     * Administrative fee = 0.5 % of the Funding Target per annum.
     * Annual KYC & AML fee = 0.1 % of the Funding Target per annum (starting year 2).
   - **Performance fee** (applicable only if the Sale Value exceeds the Funding Target or the original share purchase price):
     * 7 % of the excess return above the total investment (net of transaction costs).

Overall, the document details how property sales are initiated, approved, and executed; how investors are kept informed and how property values are periodically assessed; and the comprehensive fee regime that applies throughout the investment lifecycle.

structure.title: STAKE FEES TO INVESTORS
structure.summary:
The excerpt outlines three key areas of the platform’s investor agreement:

1. **Information and Valuation Updates (Section 13)** – Investors receive regular updates on their investment through a dashboard, including statements of income and expenses, the latest third‑party property valuation, and Sharia‑compliance audit confirmations. Valuations are refreshed semi‑annually (or at the end of a renovation period for “Fix n Flip” projects) using updated market data and independent consultants.

2. **Fee Structure (Section 14)** – A detailed schedule of charges is disclosed:
   - One‑time transaction fees: acquisition fee (1.5% of funding target), upfront KYC/AML fee (0.20%), exit fee (2.5% of the greater of funding target or sale value), and a 2.5% transfer fee on secondary sales.
   - Ongoing annual fees deducted from dividend distributions: administrative fee (0.5% of funding target) and annual KYC/AML fee (0.1%) from the second year onward.
   - Performance fee of 7% on any excess return above the funding target or original purchase price, except for sales executed during designated exit windows.
   - Additional seller fees (0%–6% of purchase price) and provisions for fee adjustments, rounding, and notice periods.

3. **Investor Representations (Section 15)** – By creating an account and using the platform, investors confirm they:
   - Have legal authority and capacity to invest, are at least 18 years old, and act in their own name (or as an authorized signatory for an entity).
   - Will provide and keep up‑to‑date accurate KYC/AML information, and acknowledge the criminal penalties for fraud or money‑laundering.
   - Are not insolvent, under legal restriction, or residing in a jurisdiction where the investment is prohibited.
   - Are acting as principals, not agents, and that the funds they deposit are legitimate.

structure.title: REPRESENTATIONS
structure.summary:
**Description of the Partial Document**

The excerpt contains two main sections:

1. **Fees (Section 14)**  
   - **Performance Fee Exception** – No performance fee is charged on sales made during “Exit Windows.”  
   - **Fee Adjustments** – Stake may change, reduce, waive, or rebate fees in line with DFSA fair‑treatment rules.  
   - **Seller‑Specific Fees** – Sellers can be charged an additional fee (0 % – 6 % of the purchase price) that depends on property details, negotiated price and other factors at the time of the investment round.  
   - **Rounding Rule** – All fees owed to Stake are rounded up to the nearest whole number.  
   - **Notice of Fee Changes** – Stake will inform investors of any fee changes at least 14 business days in advance via email.

2. **User Representations (Section 15)**  
   - **Legal Capacity & Authority** – Users must be legally entitled and authorized to invest, be at least 18 years old (if an individual), and not be insolvent, bankrupt or otherwise restricted.  
   - **Accurate Information & KYC/AML Compliance** – Users must provide and keep up‑to‑date, truthful KYC/AML details (name, DOB, residence, source of funds, etc.) and agree that fraud or misrepresentation is a criminal offense under DIFC/UAE law.  
   - **Source of Funds & Prohibited Use** – Funds must be legitimate; the platform cannot be used for illegal activities, money‑laundering or market abuse.  
   - **Account Security & Confidentiality** – Users must safeguard their username/password, maintain cybersecurity measures, and are liable for any unauthorized access.  
   - **Acknowledgment of Risks** – Users must read and accept the “Key Risks” each time they invest, and assume all associated risks, losses, and benefits.  
   - **Investor Shares & Transfer Restrictions** – Investments will be held as Investor Shares in a protected company (PC); users cannot assign, sell, or transfer their Stake account.  
   - **Compliance & Indemnification** – False or misleading representations allow Stake to terminate the account, rescind agreements, possibly return remaining funds (minus charges), and seek indemnity for any losses. Non‑compliance with KYC/AML may lead to reporting to authorities and withholding of funds.  
   - **Termination & Share Sale** – Upon termination (e.g., suspected fraud, regulatory breach, or security issues), Stake may, where permitted, arrange a private sale or exit‑window sale of the user’s Investor Shares.

structure.title: TRANSFERS
structure.summary: The excerpt contains no content, so no main points can be identified.

structure.title: TERMINATION AND VARIATION
CONTINGENCY PLANNING
structure.summary:
**Description of the Partial Document**

The excerpt outlines key contractual provisions governing the relationship between the platform “Stake” and its users (Investors). It is organized into several sections:

1. **Termination and Variation (Section 17)**
   - The agreement starts upon signing and continues indefinitely until terminated under specific events such as Stake’s insolvency, Stake’s unilateral termination with 30 days’ notice, material breach by the Investor, withdrawal of funds and closure of the Stake account, or the sale of the underlying property/investor shares.
   - Investors who hold shares in a private company (PC) retain ownership and rights to those shares after termination, subject to the transfer mechanisms detailed in earlier clauses.
   - Stake reserves the right to amend the agreement for legal or other reasons, providing a 14‑day written notice (via email or the platform). Continued platform use signals acceptance of the amended terms; the latest version prevails over prior versions.

2. **Contingency Planning (Section 18)**
   - **Business Continuity Plan (BCP):** Activated after a force‑majeure event that disrupts platform operations, aiming to maintain critical activities, protect data, keep investors informed, and restore normal service. Investors may request the BCP in writing.
   - **Cessation Plan (CP):** Triggered if Stake must limit, suspend, or permanently cease operations. The CP details procedures for administering investments and handling liquidation.
   - If investors vote (simple majority) for liquidation under the CP, an appointed administrator will sell the property, repay capital to investors proportionally, and deduct liquidation costs. Stake disclaims liability for any capital loss arising from the sale.

3. **Limitation of Liability (Section 19)**
   - Stake’s role is limited to providing a platform; it gives no investment advice or performance guarantees.
   - Stake is not liable for any direct, indirect, or consequential losses, damages, or expenses incurred by investors from using the platform, except where expressly required by the agreement or DIFC/DFSA law.
   - Financial projections and return estimates are illustrative only; Stake disclaims responsibility for any reliance on such information.

4. **Indemnity (Section 20)**
   - Investors must defend, indemnify, and hold harmless Stake and its affiliates (“Stake Indemnified Parties”) against all claims, losses, damages, fines, legal fees, etc., arising from: (a) the investor’s breach of the agreement, (b) third‑party disputes involving the investor, (c) violations of applicable laws, or (d) any act/omission by the investor that creates civil or criminal liability.
   - These indemnity obligations survive termination of the agreement.

5. **Regulatory Disclosures (Section 21)**
   - By default, investors are classified as Retail Clients under DFSA rules. Eligible investors may request reclassification as Professional Clients, which reduces certain consumer protections.
   - Stake does not perform credit checks on sellers or investors and does not provide direct investment advice.

Overall, the excerpt details how the agreement may be ended or amended, the platforms’ emergency and cessation procedures, the scope of Stake’s liability, the investor’s indemnification duties, and regulatory classification and disclosure requirements.

structure.title: LIMITATION OF LIABILITY
structure.summary:
The excerpt outlines key contractual provisions for users of the Stake platform:

* **Limitation of Liability (Section 19)** – Stake’s role is limited to providing a platform; it makes no performance guarantees, professional advice, or warranties about investment results. Users assume all investment risk, and Stake disclaims liability for any direct, indirect or consequential losses arising from platform use or reliance on financial projections.

* **Indemnity (Section 20)** – Users must defend, indemnify and hold harmless Stake and its affiliates against any claims, losses, damages, fines or legal costs stemming from breaches of the agreement, third‑party disputes, legal violations, or any wrongful acts or omissions by the user. These obligations survive termination.

* **Regulatory Disclosures (Section 21)** – Stake classifies investors as Retail Clients by default, with an option to be re‑classified as Professional Clients (which reduces protections). Stake does not assess creditworthiness of sellers or investors, provides no investment advice, and expects users to conduct their own due diligence. Potential conflicts of interest must be identified and disclosed; Stake may have fee‑sharing arrangements with brokers or other third parties, but does not receive remuneration from anyone other than sellers or investors using the platform. DFSA does not verify sellers, properties, or related information.

* **Feedback and Complaints (Section 22)** – Stake encourages feedback and details its complaints handling process: acknowledgment within 7 days, investigation by a designated senior staff member, resolution aim within 60 days, and possible escalation to regulators or arbitrators (with limited data disclosure). Users can submit complaints via email and may request the complaint handling procedures.

structure.title: INDEMNITY
structure.summary:
The excerpt comprises four key sections of the platform’s terms and conditions:

1. **Conflict‑of‑Interest and Fee Disclosures (Section 21)** – Outlines Stake’s obligations to identify, manage or disclose any actual or potential conflicts of interest with investors, describes its relationships with brokers, agents and other third parties, and clarifies that Stake’s remuneration comes solely from sellers or investors using the platform. It also states that the DFSA does not verify the sellers or the property information.

2. **Feedback and Complaints Procedure (Section 22)** – Encourages user feedback, details how complaints are to be lodged (via email), and sets out the mandatory escalation to senior management, acknowledgement timelines (within 7 days), investigation and resolution timeframe (up to 60 days), possible disclosure of personal data to regulators, and the right of the complainant to request the complaint‑handling policy.

3. **General Legal Terms (Section 23)** – Covers standard contract provisions such as non‑waiver of rights, severability of invalid clauses, absence of partnership or joint‑venture creation, formal notice requirements (hand delivery, courier, post, email), language of communication (English), exclusive jurisdiction of DIFC courts, and the integration clause stating that this agreement and related documents constitute the entire agreement.

4. **Sharia‑Compliant Requirements (Section 24)** – Declares that any interest‑based obligations are prohibited under Sharia law, confirms that the terms are drafted to meet Sharia‑h standards as interpreted by the Shariah Supervisory Board (SSB), and notes that where applicable the DIFC law governs unless it conflicts with Sharia principles.

structure.title: REGULATORY DISCLOSURES
structure.summary:
The excerpt contains the contract’s “General Terms” (Section 23), “Shariah Compliance” provisions (Section 24) and a “Contacts” clause (Section 25).  

**General Terms (23)**  
- Reiterates that any failure to enforce rights or to insist on strict performance does not waive those rights (23.1‑23.2).  
- Provides a severability clause: if any provision is held invalid or unlawful by a competent authority, it is removed while the remainder stays enforceable (23.3).  
- States that the agreement does not create a joint venture, partnership, or agency relationship between the parties (23.4).  
- Sets out formal notice requirements: all notices must be written, delivered by hand, prepaid courier, post, or email to the registered address/email; defines when a notice is deemed served (23.5).  
- Requires all communications to be in English (23.6).  
- Specifies that any dispute will be governed by DIFC law and resolved exclusively by the DIFC Courts, with an initial complaint‑filing step (23.7).  
- Declares the agreement, together with the Administration Agreement and any referenced documents, to be the entire agreement, superseding prior discussions or writings (23.8).  

**Shariah Compliance (24)**  
- Confirms that payment of interest is prohibited under Shariah; the parties expressly waive any right to claim or pay interest (24.1).  
- Notes that the terms have been drafted to comply with Shariah as interpreted by the Shariah Supervisory Board (SSB); parties agree to be bound by the SSB’s rulings (24.2).  
- Provides that for Shariah‑compliant investments, clause 3.7 and DIFC law apply unless they conflict with Shariah principles, in which case the SSB’s interpretation prevails (24.3).  

**Contacts (25)**  
- Requires all notices, communications, suggestions, queries, or complaints under the agreement to be in English and sent via email to contact@getstake.com.  

Overall, the passage outlines fundamental contractual mechanics (waiver, severability, notice, governing law, entire agreement), embeds mandatory Shariah compliance (prohibiting interest and deferring to the SSB), and supplies the designated contact channel for correspondence.

structure.title: FEEDBACK AND COMPLAINTS
structure.summary: The excerpt is an instruction stating that the response should consist solely of the requested description, without any additional wording or content.

structure.title: GENERAL TERMS
structure.summary:
**Description of the Partial Document**

The excerpt is a section of a comprehensive legal agreement governing the relationship between Stake (a DIFC‑registered platform) and its investors. It details the circumstances under which Stake may force the sale or exit of an investor’s shares, the procedures for handling investments that become non‑Sharia‑compliant, and the rights and obligations of the parties in such events.

Key topics covered include:

1. **Grounds for Forced Sale / Exit** – Investor shares may be sold or the investment exited if the investor fails to provide required AML/KYC information, breaches sanctions, violates the agreement, or does not comply with required notifications.

2. **Sharia‑Non‑Compliant Investments** – Stake must attempt to remediate non‑compliance within 60 days; if unsuccessful, investors may exit without penalty (subject to applicable fees), and Stake will arrange a sale or vote to liquidate the entire investment.

3. **Liability for Sale Proceeds** – Stake limits its liability for actions taken under the forced‑sale clauses and reserves the right to deduct amounts owed to Stake or required by law from any proceeds.

4. **Transfer of Investor Shares** – Exhaustive list of permissible transfer scenarios (majority vote sale, incapacity, family transfers, corporate insolvency, exit‑window transfers). Detailed mechanism separating economic ownership from legal title during the “Beneficial Assignment Period” after a transaction is confirmed.

5. **Termination & Variation** – Conditions under which the agreement may end (insolvency, material breach, withdrawal of funds, completion of sale). Stake’s right to amend the agreement with notice, and the continuation of rights over existing shares after termination.

6. **Contingency Planning** – Business Continuity Plan (BCP) for platform failures and Cessation Plan (CP) for permanent shutdown, including liquidation procedures and investor voting.

7. **Limitation of Liability & Disclaimer** – Stake provides no performance guarantees, disclaims liability for losses or damages arising from platform use, and clarifies that financial projections are not guaranteed.

8. **Indemnity** – Investors must indemnify Stake and its affiliates against any claims, losses, or legal actions stemming from breaches, third‑party disputes, or regulatory violations.

9. **Regulatory Disclosures** – Classification of investors (retail vs. professional), lack of credit checks or advisory services, conflict‑of‑interest management, and fee structures.

10. **Feedback & Complaints Procedure** – Process for submitting complaints, timelines for acknowledgment and investigation, escalation to senior management, and potential regulatory disclosure.

11. **General Terms** – Waiver provisions, severability, non‑creation of joint ventures, notice requirements, governing law (DIFC), dispute resolution (DIFC Courts), and integration clause.

12. **Sharia Compliance** – Waiver of interest, adherence to SSB‑issued Sharia rulings, and supremacy of Sharia over DIFC law where conflicts arise.

13. **Contact Information** – Email address for all communications, feedback, and complaints.

14. **Appendix 1 – Administration Agreement (partial)** – Introduction of the administration agreement between Stake and a prescribed special‑purpose vehicle (the “Company”), including parties, recitals, definitions, and the engagement clause that appoints Stake to administer the Company’s affairs, with details on director appointments and the transfer of ownership to investors upon funding.

Overall, the document sets out the legal framework for investment, share transfer, compliance, termination, risk allocation, and governance of the Stake platform and its related special‑purpose vehicles.

structure.title: SHARIAH COMPLIANCE
structure.summary:
The excerpt is a segment of an Administration Agreement governing the relationship between a newly incorporated company (the “Company”) and a service provider, Stake. It begins with a series of definitions covering key terms such as “Act,” “Articles,” “Business Day,” “Confidential Information,” “Personal Data,” “Process,” “Property,” “Sales Taxes,” “Services,” and “Shareholders,” which set the interpretive framework for the contract.  

The agreement then outlines the engagement: Stake is appointed to deliver specific administrative services (listed in Schedule 1) to the Company, which becomes effective upon incorporation. It specifies the initial ownership structure (Stake owning the purpose‑carrying (PC) entity and appointing two directors) and the mechanism for transferring ownership to investors once a funding target is reached, including rights to replace directors.  

The term and termination clause states that the agreement runs from the execution date until the Company sells the Property.  

Company obligations are detailed, requiring the Company to facilitate Stake’s service delivery, grant access to the Property, provide accurate information, maintain insurance (with Stake’s assistance), and pay all applicable fees.  

Finally, the fee schedule is described: the Company must pay ongoing administrative, KYC/AML, and other third‑party expenses to Stake, subject to UAE VAT. Specific fee examples are given, including an annual administration fee of 0.50 % of revenue, variable channel fees of 0–20 % of annual income, and property management fees (fixed AED 1,750–4,000 or variable 4–5 % of annual income).

structure.title: CONTACTS
structure.summary:
The excerpt is a formal **Administration Agreement** between a company (the “Company”) and a service provider called Stake. It outlines the key responsibilities, fees, and procedural rules governing Stake’s administration of the Company’s property until the property is sold. The main points covered are:

1. **Good‑faith performance** – Stake must use reasonable efforts to assist the Company in administering the property as described in Schedule 1.  
2. **Term** – The agreement starts on the execution date and ends when the property is sold.  
3. **Company obligations** – The Company must facilitate Stake’s work, grant access to the property, provide accurate information, maintain adequate insurance, and pay all fees and charges.  
4. **Fees and expenses** –  
   - Ongoing administrative, KYC/AML, and property‑management fees (specified as percentages of income or fixed AED amounts).  
   - All fees are subject to UAE VAT.  
   - The Company also reimburses third‑party costs incurred by Stake (utilities, repairs, valuations, etc.).  
   - Invoicing, payment, and documentation requirements are set out.  
5. **Material property costs** – If a cost exceeds 5 % of the property price and cannot be covered by rental income, Stake notifies the Company; Stake may pre‑pay the amount and be reimbursed from rental income, shareholder capital, or other revenue, in a Sharia‑compliant manner.  
6. **Legal and compliance** – Stake warrants that it will maintain all necessary licenses and regulatory approvals and provide proof of compliance on request.  
7. **Confidentiality** – Both parties must keep the agreement and related information confidential, with detailed permitted disclosures (employees, advisors, legal obligations, investors, potential buyers, etc.) and post‑termination handling of confidential documents.  
8. **Assignment** – Stake may assign or subcontract its rights and obligations with seven days’ notice, provided the assignee meets Stake’s standards.  
9. **Amendment/termination** – Any changes require written agreement (including email) signed by both parties under a simple majority vote; the clause begins to outline the amendment process.

Overall, the document sets out the framework for how Stake will manage the Company’s property, the financial arrangements involved, compliance expectations, confidentiality safeguards, and the mechanisms for modifying or ending the agreement.

structure.title: APPENDIX 1 – ADMINISTRATION AGREEMENT
structure.summary:
The excerpt is a set of contractual clauses covering the following key areas:

1. **Confidentiality (Clause 7)** – Defines “Confidential Information” and obliges both parties to keep it secret, limiting use to performance of the agreement. Lists permissible disclosures (e.g., to employees, advisors, legal authorities, investors, potential buyers, etc.) and sets out post‑termination duties to destroy or return confidential materials, with survival of the confidentiality obligations.

2. **Assignment (Clause 8)** – Allows Stake to assign, transfer or subcontract its rights and obligations after a 7‑day written notice, provided the assignee meets quality and regulatory standards and does not prejudice the other party’s rights.

3. **Termination and Variation (Clause 9)** – Provides that any amendment must be in writing (emails included) and approved by a simple majority vote, with changes communicated to investors. Either party may terminate the agreement with 90 days’ written notice.

4. **Validity of Agreement (Clause 10)** – Contains mutual warranties that each party has the legal capacity and authority to enter the agreement and that the agreement is legally binding.

5. **Notices (Clause 11)** – Specifies how formal notices must be delivered (hand, courier, or email), the addresses to use, and when notice is deemed served.

6. **Miscellaneous Provisions (Clause 12)** – Covers a broad range of standard boilerplate matters, including:
   - Requirement for written amendments.
   - Entire‑agreement clause superseding prior arrangements.
   - No implied terms, reliance on written provisions only, and no creation of a partnership or joint venture.
   - No third‑party enforcement rights unless expressly provided.
   - Severability of invalid provisions.
   - Non‑waiver of lawful rights.
   - Ability to extend deadlines by mutual written agreement.
   - Preservation of other legal rights and remedies.
   - Binding effect on successors and permitted assignees.
   - Execution in counterparts.
   - Governing law (DIFC law) and exclusive jurisdiction of the DIFC Courts.

7. **Signature Block** – Concludes with spaces for the Company and Stake Properties Limited to sign and date the agreement.

structure.title: SCHEDULE 1 – DESCRIPTION OF SERVICES
structure.summary: The excerpt is Schedule 1 of an Administration Agreement that sets out a non‑exhaustive list of services the provider (Stake) must perform for the Company and its Property. It details administrative responsibilities such as maintaining statutory books, filing regulatory documents and communicating with authorities; managing and supervising voting processes through a platform; acting as the Company’s agent for property‑management agreements; gathering, aggregating and reporting property and company information to investors; administering the Company’s funds for day‑to‑day operations (rent collection, expense payments, dividend distribution); procuring appropriate insurance coverage; engaging valuation consultants when needed; arranging for an agent to sell the Property upon instruction; liaising with the Property Manager; and any other routine administrative tasks required under the agreement.

structure.title: APPENDIX 2 – REVISED STANDARD ARTICLES OF ASSOCIATION FOR PRIVATE COMPANIES
structure.summary:
The excerpt is a complete, draft set of “Revised Standard Articles of Association” for a private company incorporated under the Dubai International Financial Centre (DIFC) Companies Law (Law No. 5 of 2018). It outlines the company’s constitution and includes the following main provisions:

1. **Interpretation & Definitions** – A comprehensive list of defined terms (e.g., Articles, Board, Directors, Shareholder, Ordinary/Special Resolution, Investment Agreement, etc.) and rules on construction, gender, singular/plural, and electronic communication.

2. **Corporate Identity** – Sections on the company’s name, registered office (DIFC), and stated business purpose – a crowdfunding structure for holding assets invested through a DFSA‑licensed platform, plus ancillary activities.

3. **Share‑holder Liability & Capital** – Limited liability of shareholders, authorised and issued share capital (normally 1 AED nominal value per ordinary share), and rules that shares cannot be issued below nominal value.

4. **Share Structure** – Details on classes of shares, rights attached to each class, issuance of share certificates, and the company’s lien on partly‑paid shares, including enforcement and sale of such shares.

5. **Calls on Shares & Forfeiture** – Powers of the directors to make calls, notice periods, penalties for non‑payment, and treatment of unpaid amounts as calls.

6. **Transfer & Transmission of Shares** – Procedures for registering transfers, directors’ discretion to refuse registration, suspension of transfers, and the handling of shares on death, bankruptcy or other transmission events.

7. **Alteration of Share Capital** – Special‑resolution powers to increase, consolidate, subdivide, or cancel shares, and provisions for reducing capital under the law.

8. **Purchase of Own Shares** – The company’s ability to buy back its own shares subject to legal requirements.

9. **General Meetings** – Calling of meetings by directors or shareholders, electronic notice (minimum 7 days, 14‑day rule for short notice), contents of notices, quorum requirements, voting procedures (ordinary vs. special resolutions), and the effect of abstentions.

10. **Voting Rights** – One vote per share, ordinary resolutions for most matters, special resolutions for material decisions (e.g., changing investment periods, selling property, DFSA‑material decisions).

11. **Board of Directors** – Minimum of one director, appointment/removal by shareholders, alternate directors, powers of the board, delegation of authority, and the ability of shareholders to direct the board by special resolution.

12. **Directors’ Governance** – Disqualification and removal grounds, remuneration, appointment to executive offices, gratuities/pensions, meeting procedures, quorum, chairperson duties, voting, and electronic participation.

13. **Conflict of Interest** – Disclosure requirements, voting rights of interested directors, and the board’s ability to relax restrictions.

14. **Company Officer** – Appointment and removal of a secretary, minute‑keeping obligations, and indemnity of directors/officers.

15. **Dividends & Financing** – Power to declare ordinary or interim dividends, distribution of assets, handling of unclaimed dividends, and the option to capitalise profits.

16. **Accounts, Audit & Inspection** – Rights of shareholders to inspect records, appointment of auditors, and confidentiality provisions.

17. **Notices** – Methods of service (personal, post, electronic, or agreed), proof of service rules, and special provisions for joint shareholders or transmitters.

18. **Indemnity & Amendment** – Company’s indemnity obligations to directors/officers and the requirement that any amendment to these Articles must be made by special resolution.

19. **Execution** – Signature block for the incorporators.

Overall, the document establishes the legal framework governing the formation, capital structure, governance, shareholder rights, and administrative procedures of a private DIFC company that operates a crowdfunding investment platform.
